sections.heading: Overview
sections.body: The Transformer architecture, introduced by Vaswani et al. (2017), revolutionized sequence modeling by replacing recurrence with self-attention mechanisms. This review covers the core architecture, key variants, and recent advances relevant to understanding modern large language models.

sections.heading: Key Concepts
bullets: Self-Attention: Mechanism that computes relevance scores between all pairs of positions in a sequence, enabling parallel processing and long-range dependencies [Vaswani et al., 2017]
Multi-Head Attention: Running multiple attention operations in parallel, each learning different relationship patterns [Vaswani et al., 2017]
Positional Encoding: Sinusoidal or learned embeddings that inject sequence order information since attention is permutation-invariant [Vaswani et al., 2017]
Layer Normalization: Stabilizes training by normalizing activations across features [Ba et al., 2016]
Feed-Forward Networks: Position-wise fully connected layers that add non-linearity between attention layers

sections.heading: Original Transformer and Foundations
sections.body: Vaswani et al. (2017) introduced the Transformer for machine translation, achieving state-of-the-art results on WMT benchmarks. The key insight was that attention alone, without recurrence, could model sequential dependencies effectively while enabling full parallelization during training.

sections.heading: Encoder-Only Models (BERT Family)
sections.body: Devlin et al. (2019) showed that pre-training a Transformer encoder with masked language modeling produces powerful general-purpose representations. BERT and its variants (RoBERTa, ALBERT, DeBERTa) became the foundation for most NLP classification and extraction tasks.

sections.heading: Decoder-Only Models (GPT Family)
sections.body: Radford et al. (2018, 2019) demonstrated that autoregressive language modeling with Transformer decoders scales predictably. GPT-2 and GPT-3 showed emergent capabilities at scale, establishing the scaling laws paradigm.

sections.heading: Discussion Points
bullets: Efficiency vs. capability: Linear attention variants (Performer, Linformer) trade some accuracy for O(n) complexity -- when is this trade-off worth it?
Scaling laws: Do Transformer capabilities continue to improve predictably with scale, or are there diminishing returns?
Interpretability: Attention weights are often cited as explanations, but research shows they don't always align with feature importance

sections.heading: Suggested Assignment Structure
bullets: Introduction: Motivation for attention-based models, limitations of RNNs/LSTMs
Background: Sequence-to-sequence models, attention mechanism evolution
The Transformer Architecture: Detailed walkthrough of components
Variants and Applications: BERT, GPT, Vision Transformer
Conclusion: Current state and future directions

sections.heading: Sources
bullets: [1] Vaswani, A. et al. (2017). Attention Is All You Need. NeurIPS. https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762
[2] Devlin, J. et al. (2019). BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers. NAACL. https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.04805
[3] Radford, A. et al. (2019). Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners. OpenAI.
[4] Dosovitskiy, A. et al. (2020). An Image is Worth 16x16 Words. ICLR. https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.11929
[5] Ba, J. et al. (2016). Layer Normalization. https://arxiv.org/abs/1607.06450
